Extending the PICO Software Framework

Software Overview

PICO provides a hierarchically-organized set of C++ capabilities that users may combine and extend to form their own applications. As with ABACUS library, extending the core capabilities of PICO requires the development of derived classes that express the additional required functionality. The following figure illustrates the inheritance tree used to develop serial and parallel applications in PICO. At the root of the inheritance tree is the PICO core, which provides basic capabilities for describing and parallelizing branch-and-bound algorithms.

PICO consists of two core software layers, the serial layer and the parallel layer. The serial layer provides an object-oriented means of describing branch-and-bound algorithms, with essentially no reference to parallel implementation. The serial layer's design has some novel features, and we expect it to be useful in its own right. For users uninterested in parallelism, or simply in the early stages of algorithm development, the serial layer allows branch-and-bound methods to be described, debugged, and run in a familiar, serial development environment.

PICO's parallel layer was designed using a distributed-memory computation model, which requires message passing to communicate information between processors. The parallel layer uses the MPI standard application interface to pass messages between processors. There are currently two portable interface standards for constructing message-passing programs, MPI and PVM. PICO uses MPI because it is designed to be customized for maximum performance on MPP systems like the ASCI supercomputers. The design of PVM stresses the ability to operate on heterogeneous platforms, at some sacrifice in performance.

Extending the PICO Core

Defining a serial branch-and-bound algorithm with PICO requires the extension of two principal classes in the PICO serial layer: branching and branchSub. The branching class stores global information about a problem instance and contains methods that implement various kinds of serial branch-and-bound algorithms. The branchSub class stores data about each subproblem in the search tree, and it contains methods that perform generic operations on subproblems.

There are a variety of abstract virtual methods in the branching and branchSub classes that must be defined to fully implement an application of the PICO serial layer:

  1. branching::blankSub - This method constructs an empty subproblem.

  2. branchSub::setRootComputation - This method initializes an empty subproblem to be the root of the branching tree.

  3. branchSub::boundComputation - This method computes the lower bound on this subproblem's value. When this routine is finished the state of the subproblem should be set to bounded or dead.

  4. branchSub::splitComputation - This method determines how many children will be generated. When this routine is finished the state of the subproblem should be set to separated.

  5. branchSub::makeChild - This method creates a child subproblem of a given subproblem.

  6. branchSub::candidateSolution - This method returns true if this subproblem represents a feasible solution.

The branching and branchSub classes also have a large number of other virtual methods that may be overridden to customize the PICO solver. The following are some of the more commonly-overridden optional virtual methods:
  1. branching::preprocess - This method performs preliminary computation before starting the search, typically performing computations that improve subsequent bounding calculations.

  2. branching::aPrioriBound - This method can be defined to specify an initial bound on the value of the best possible solution.

  3. branching::initialGuess - This method can be defined to specify the value of an initial incumbent value.

  4. branching::haveIncumbentHeuristic - This method returns true if an incumbent heuristic is implemented in incumbentHeuristic.

  5. branching::serialPrintSolution - This method writes the incumbent solution to an output stream.

  6. branchSub::candidateSolution - If this method returns true, PICO assumes that the computed bound is in fact the objective value of the best feasible solution within the portion of the search space corresponding to the subproblem. If this bound is better than the current incumbent, the handler calls updateIncumbent to replace the current incumbent with the solution corresponding to the subproblem. By default, updateIncumbent simply stores the corresponding objective value; in most applications, the user will override this function to also store an application-specific representation of the solution for possible later output.

  7. branchSub::incumbentHeuristic - This method provides a way to execute an application-specific heuristic on subproblems during the search. If this method finds a better incumbent solution, then it can call updateIncumbent. PICO calls incumbentHeuristic for a subproblem if the method candidateSolution returns false and the method haveIncumbentHeuristic returns true.

Parallelizing a branch-and-bound application developed with the serial layer requires the extension of the parallelBranching and parallelBranchSub in addition to the extensions for branching and branchSub. A fully-operational parallel application requires only the definition of a few additional methods for these derived classes, principally to tell PICO how to pack application-specific problem and subproblem data into MPI message buffers, and later unpack them. Any parallel PICO application constructed in this way inherits the full capabilities of the parallel layer, including a wide range of different parallel work distribution and load balancing strategies, and user-configurable levels of interprocessor communication. Application-specific refinements to the parallelization can then be added, but are not required.

The following abstract virtual methods in the parallelBranching and parallelBranchSub classes must be defined to fully implement an application of the PICO parallel layer:

  1. parallelBranching::pack - This method packs application-specific global problem information into a buffer.

  2. parallelBranching::unpack - This method unpacks application-specific global problem information from a buffer.

  3. parallelBranching::spPackSize - This method returns an estimate of the maximum buffer space needed to pack the application-specific portion of subproblems.

  4. parallelBranching::blankParallelSub - This method constructs an empty subproblem.

  5. parallelBranchSub::pack - This method packs application-specific subproblem data into a buffer.

  6. parallelBranchSub::unpack - This method unpacks application-specific subproblem data from a buffer.

  7. parallelBranchSub::parallelMakeChild - This methods constructs a single child of a given subproblem. (This is similar to branchSub::makeChild, but returns an object of type parallelBranchSub.)
